question: What color is MOST associated with St. Patrick's Day?
hint: Think of shamrocks! Though blue was actually St. Patrick's original color.

question: In what month does St. Patrick's Day take place?
hint: St. Patrick's Day is March 17th!

question: What three-leafed plant did St. Patrick use to teach the Holy Trinity?
hint: St. Patrick used it to explain Father, Son, and Holy Spirit as one God.

question: What does 'Erin Go Bragh' mean?
hint: It comes from Irish 'Éire go Brách' — Ireland until the end of time!

question: What country is St. Patrick the patron saint of?
hint: He drove the snakes out — well, legend says so!

question: How many leaves does a shamrock have?
hint: A four-leaf clover is rare and lucky, but a shamrock specifically has three.

question: What color is traditionally worn on St. Patrick's Day?
hint: You'll get pinched if you forget to wear it!

question: What does 'céilí' mean?
hint: It's a traditional Irish social gathering with music and dancing.

question: On which streets does the Scranton St. Patrick's Parade march?
hint: Look at the street signs right here at the parade!

question: What is anthracite?
hint: Scranton was built on it — the Wyoming Valley is famous for it.

question: What is a leprechaun said to guard?
hint: Find the end of the rainbow and you might catch one!

question: What is 'pub' short for?
hint: It's a place open to the public — a house for socializing.

question: During the Great Famine, Irish people fled which country?
hint: The potato blight devastated their homeland in the mid-1800s.

question: What does Steamtown National Historic Site preserve?
hint: Those big iron locomotives are a clue!

question: What does 'Erin' mean in Irish?
hint: It's a poetic name for the island itself.

question: Where was St. Patrick originally from before going to Ireland?
hint: He was born around 385 AD in Roman Britain, likely in Wales or Scotland.

question: What are the three colors of the Irish flag?
hint: Green for Catholics, Orange for Protestants, White for peace between them.

question: Why is Scranton called the 'Electric City'?
hint: On November 30, 1886, Scranton became the first US city to run electric streetcars.

question: What musical instrument is central to traditional Irish music?
hint: The fiddle is the heartbeat of every céilí and session!

question: What year did Scranton first run electric streetcars?
hint: The Scranton Suburban Railway made history that autumn.

question: In which years did the Great Irish Famine occur?
hint: A potato blight caused massive starvation and emigration to America.

question: What is the city of Scranton named after?
options: The Scranton family of mine owners
hint: George and Selden Scranton founded the city in the 1840s.

question: What year was Scranton's first St. Patrick's Day Parade?
hint: It began the same year JFK was president — and hasn't stopped since!

question: What did anthracite coal primarily power in the 1800s–1900s?
hint: It burned hot and clean — perfect for heating homes and running furnaces.

question: What did Irish immigrants build in Scranton and across Pennsylvania?
hint: They came with strong backs and built the whole industrial foundation.

question: What is 'Top o' the mornin'' ?
hint: The full phrase is 'Top o' the mornin' to ye!' — a cheery hello.

question: What is a 'bodhrán'?
hint: It's a shallow drum played with a tipper and heard at every session.

question: St. Patrick's Day falls on March what?
hint: It's the traditional date of St. Patrick's death in 461 AD.

question: What is the Wyoming Valley historically known for?
hint: The valley sits on one of the world's largest anthracite deposits.

question: Approximately what was Scranton's population at its peak (around 1930)?
hint: The mining and railroad boom brought tens of thousands of immigrants.

question: In what year was St. Peter's Cathedral in Scranton completed?
hint: It took decades to build and was completed in the 1880s.

question: What does 'Sláinte' mean?
hint: Raise your glass and say it — it's the Irish/Scottish word for health.

question: Many Scranton Irish emigrated from which counties in Ireland?
hint: The western counties were hit hardest by the Famine.

question: What was St. Patrick's birth name?
hint: He took the name Patricius (Patrick) after becoming a bishop.

question: What does the orange stripe in the Irish flag represent?
hint: William of Orange was the Protestant king — that's the connection.

question: What does 'craic' mean in Irish slang?
hint: 'Great craic!' means a fantastic time was had by all.

question: What year did Steamtown become a National Historic Site?
hint: It was designated the same year as the Statue of Liberty centennial.

question: What does 'Nay Aug' mean in the Lenape language?
hint: The Nay Aug Gorge in Scranton takes its name from the original inhabitants.

question: What was the full name of Scranton's first electric streetcar line?
hint: It ran from Scranton to the suburbs — revolutionary in 1886.

question: What nickname did Scranton have BEFORE being called 'Electric City'?
hint: Iron manufacturing came before the electric streetcar era.

question: Who founded the city of Scranton?
hint: Brothers who brought the iron industry to the Lackawanna Valley in the 1840s.

question: Who were the Molly Maguires?
options: A secret Irish miners' resistance group
hint: They fought dangerous working conditions in the coal mines — controversially.

question: What year was the Delaware, Lackawanna and Western Railroad founded?
hint: It connected the coal regions to New York and was an industrial giant.

question: What does the Great Seal of Ireland depict?
hint: The harp is Ireland's national emblem — it even appears on euro coins.

question: What does the Irish phrase 'Póg mo thóin' humorously translate to?
hint: It's a cheeky phrase — probably best saved for close friends!

question: What is the Brian P. Kelly Memorial Footrace?
options: A St. Patrick's Day morning race in Scranton
hint: It kicks off St. Patrick's Day morning right here in Scranton, PA!

question: What time does the Brian P. Kelly Memorial Footrace start on Parade Day?
hint: The race starts a couple of hours before the noon parade!

question: What year was the Brian P. Kelly Memorial Footrace established?
hint: It was founded in the late 1970s to honor a beloved community figure.

question: What famous running event is held in Dublin, Ireland on St. Patrick's Day weekend?
hint: Dublin hosts a major road race every St. Patrick's Day — a 10K through the city!

question: Who was Brian P. Kelly, namesake of the Scranton footrace?
options: A beloved local community figure and longtime parade participant
hint: He was known and loved throughout the Scranton community.

question: Approximately how long is the Brian P. Kelly Memorial Footrace course?
hint: A 5K is a popular race distance — about 3.1 miles.

question: What famous nickname appears on Scranton's iconic Lackawanna Avenue sign?
hint: Scranton earned the name by pioneering electric streetcars in America.

question: What type of transportation gave Scranton the 'Electric City' nickname?
hint: It ran on rails right down the street — powered by electricity, not horses.

question: Scranton launched one of America's first electric streetcar systems in which decade?
hint: It was the same decade the Statue of Liberty was unveiled (1886).

question: What color does the famous Electric City sign glow on St. Patrick's Day?
hint: It's dyed the color of the Emerald Isle for the holiday!

question: The word 'Lackawanna' comes from a Lenape phrase meaning what?
hint: The Delaware people named many rivers and valleys in this region.

question: What industry first brought large numbers of Irish immigrants to the Lackawanna Valley?
hint: The region's hard coal veins were the backbone of 19th-century Scranton.
